annotations.label: Algebraic number field

anchor.snippet: an algebraic number field (or simply number field ) is an extension field

annotations.note: `NumberField` is a field of characteristic zero that is finite-dimensional over ℚ, matching the notion of a finite extension of ℚ.

annotations.label: Field

anchor.snippet: A field consists of a set of elements together with two operations

annotations.note: `Field` extends `CommRing` and `DivisionRing`, capturing the two operations with inverses for nonzero elements.

annotations.label: Finite-dimensional vector space

anchor.snippet: the vector space is said to be of finite dimension

annotations.note: `FiniteDimensional K V` is an abbreviation for `Module.Finite K V`, i.e. a finitely generated (finite-dimensional) vector space.

annotations.label: Number field (definition)

anchor.snippet: is a finite- degree field extension of the field of rational numbers

annotations.note: `NumberField` is defined as a field finite-dimensional over ℚ (with char zero, automatic), the finite-degree extension condition.

annotations.label: Rational numbers as smallest number field

anchor.snippet: The smallest and most basic number field is the field

annotations.note: The instance `Rat.numberField` shows ℚ is a number field, though its minimality is only an informal remark.

annotations.label: Gaussian rationals

anchor.snippet: form the first (historically) non-trivial example of a number field

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Gaussian integers ℤ[i] (`GaussianInt`) but not the Gaussian rationals ℚ(i) as a named field object.

annotations.label: Nonzero Gaussian rationals are invertible

anchor.snippet: Non-zero Gaussian rational numbers are invertible

annotations.note: ℚ(i) arises as the fraction field of ℤ[i] (a field by the general fraction-field construction), but isn't a named declaration and this specific invertibility claim is not stated.

anchors.snippet: Non-zero Gaussian rational numbers are invertible

annotations.label: Gaussian rationals are two-dimensional number field

anchor.snippet: It follows that the Gaussian rationals form a number field that is two-dimensional as a vector space over

annotations.note: No named Gaussian rationals field exists, so its 2-dimensionality over ℚ is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: It follows that the Gaussian rationals form a number field that is two-dimensional as a vector space over

annotations.label: Quadratic field

anchor.snippet: is a number field obtained by adjoining the square root

annotations.note: Mathlib has ℤ[√d] (`Zsqrtd`) and general field adjunction (`AdjoinRoot`), but no dedicated quadratic number field ℚ(√d).

annotations.label: Cyclotomic field

anchor.snippet: by adjoining a primitive n -th root of unity

annotations.note: `CyclotomicField n K` is the splitting field of the n-th cyclotomic polynomial, i.e. K adjoined a primitive n-th root of unity.

annotations.label: Reals and complexes are not number fields

anchor.snippet: hence, they are not number fields

annotations.note: No statement that ℝ or ℂ fail to be number fields (being infinite-dimensional over ℚ) is recorded.

annotations.label: Pairs of rationals is not a field

anchor.snippet: However, it is not a field, since it has zero divisors

annotations.note: The specific fact that ℚ × ℚ has zero divisors and is not a field is not a recorded result.

annotations.label: Algebraic field extension

anchor.snippet: is algebraic if every element

annotations.note: `Algebra.IsAlgebraic R A` holds iff every element of A is algebraic over R.

annotations.label: Finite-degree extensions are algebraic

anchor.snippet: Every field extension of finite degree is algebraic.

annotations.note: `Algebra.IsAlgebraic.of_finite` shows a module-finite algebra is algebraic.

annotations.label: Monic polynomial

anchor.snippet: A polynomial with this property is known as a monic polynomial .

annotations.note: `Polynomial.Monic p` means the leading coefficient of p equals 1.

annotations.label: Algebraic integer

anchor.snippet: is called an algebraic integer

annotations.note: An algebraic integer is `IsIntegral ℤ x`, i.e. a root of a monic integer polynomial.

annotations.label: Every integer is an algebraic integer

anchor.snippet: is an algebraic integer, as it is the zero of the linear monic polynomial

annotations.note: `isIntegral_algebraMap` gives that every base-ring element (in particular each integer) is integral.

annotations.label: Rational algebraic integer is an integer

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that any algebraic integer that is also a rational number must actually be an integer

annotations.note: ℤ is integrally closed (via `GCDMonoid.toIsIntegrallyClosed`), so a rational integral over ℤ lies in ℤ.

annotations.label: Sum and product of algebraic integers

anchor.snippet: the sum and the product of any two algebraic integers is still an algebraic integer

annotations.note: `IsIntegral.add` and `IsIntegral.mul` show integrality is preserved by sums and products.

annotations.label: Ring of integers

anchor.snippet: called the ring of integers of

annotations.note: `NumberField.RingOfIntegers K` is the integral closure of ℤ in K.

annotations.label: Ring of integers is an integral domain

anchor.snippet: A field contains no zero divisors and this property is inherited by any subring

annotations.note: `RingOfIntegers` is declared `deriving IsDomain`, reflecting that a subring of a field has no zero divisors.

annotations.label: Number field is field of fractions of its ring of integers

anchor.snippet: is the field of fractions of the integral domain

annotations.note: The instance `[NumberField K] : IsFractionRing (𝓞 K) K` makes K the field of fractions of its ring of integers.

annotations.label: Dedekind ring

anchor.snippet: is called a Dedekind ring (or Dedekind domain )

annotations.note: `IsDedekindDomain` formalizes Dedekind domains, and `𝓞 K` is given this instance.

annotations.label: Unique factorization of ideals

anchor.snippet: there is a unique factorization of ideals into a product of prime ideals

annotations.note: `Ideal.uniqueFactorizationMonoid` gives unique factorization of ideals of a Dedekind domain into primes.

annotations.label: Factorization of an ideal into prime ideals

anchor.snippet: of quadratic integers factors into prime ideals as

annotations.note: This particular numerical ideal factorization example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Failure of unique factorization in quadratic integers

anchor.snippet: the uniqueness of the factorization fails

annotations.note: The specific failure of unique factorization in a quadratic integer ring is not formalized as an example.

annotations.label: Euclidean domains are unique factorization domains

anchor.snippet: Euclidean domains are unique factorization domains

annotations.note: Euclidean domains are PIDs (`EuclideanDomain.to_principal_ideal_domain`), and PIDs are UFDs (`PrincipalIdealRing.to_uniqueFactorizationMonoid`).

annotations.label: Ideal class group is finite

anchor.section: Analytic objects: ζ-functions, L -functions, and class number formula
anchor.snippet: This group is always finite

annotations.note: The `Fintype (ClassGroup (𝓞 K))` instance establishes finiteness of the class group of a number field.

annotations.label: Unique factorization iff class number one

anchor.section: Analytic objects: ζ-functions, L -functions, and class number formula
anchor.snippet: possesses unique factorization if and only if it is a principal ring

annotations.note: `classNumber_eq_one_iff` states class number 1 ↔ `𝓞 K` is a principal ideal ring (equivalent to unique factorization).

annotations.label: Dedekind zeta function

anchor.section: Analytic objects: ζ-functions, L -functions, and class number formula
anchor.snippet: It involves the Dedekind zeta function

annotations.note: `NumberField.dedekindZeta` defines the Dedekind zeta function of a number field.

annotations.label: Class number formula

anchor.section: Analytic objects: ζ-functions, L -functions, and class number formula
anchor.snippet: The class number formula states that

annotations.note: This theorem gives the residue of the Dedekind zeta function at s=1, the analytic class number formula.

annotations.label: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions

anchor.section: Analytic objects: ζ-functions, L -functions, and class number formula
anchor.snippet: Dirichlet's theorem asserts that in any arithmetic progression

annotations.note: `Nat.infinite_setOf_prime_and_eq_mod` proves there are infinitely many primes in each invertible residue class mod q.

annotations.label: Integral basis

anchor.snippet: An integral basis for a number field

annotations.note: `RingOfIntegers.basis` is a ℤ-basis of `𝓞 K`, i.e. an integral basis.

annotations.label: Power basis

anchor.snippet: there are particular ones known as power bases, that are bases of the form

annotations.note: `PowerBasis R S` bundles a generator whose powers form a basis.

annotations.label: Primitive element theorem

anchor.snippet: By the primitive element theorem , there exists such an

annotations.note: `Field.exists_primitive_element` proves a finite separable extension has a primitive element.

anchors.snippet: By the primitive element theorem , there exists such an

annotations.label: Power integral basis and monogenic field

anchor.snippet: is called a power integral basis

annotations.note: Power-basis machinery exists, but there is no dedicated 'power integral basis of 𝓞 K' or 'monogenic field' predicate.

anchors.snippet: is called a power integral basis

annotations.label: Non-monogenic field (Dedekind's example)

anchor.snippet: An example of a number field that is not monogenic was first given by Dedekind

annotations.note: Dedekind's specific non-monogenic field example is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: An example of a number field that is not monogenic was first given by Dedekind

annotations.label: Regular representation

anchor.snippet: is called the regular representation

annotations.note: `Algebra.lmul R A : A →ₐ[R] End R A` sends x to left-multiplication, the regular representation.

annotations.label: Trace and norm of a field element

anchor.snippet: is called the trace of the field element

annotations.note: `Algebra.trace` (and `Algebra.norm`) give the trace/norm as the trace/determinant of the multiplication map.

anchors.snippet: is called the trace of the field element

annotations.label: Trace and norm computation

anchor.snippet: We proceed to calculate the trace

annotations.note: This specific worked trace/norm computation is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: We proceed to calculate the trace

annotations.label: Linearity of trace and multiplicativity of norm

anchor.snippet: Tr( x ) is a linear function of x

annotations.note: `Algebra.trace` is bundled as an R-linear map and `Algebra.norm` as a monoid hom, encoding linearity and multiplicativity.

annotations.label: Trace form

anchor.snippet: The trace form derived is a bilinear form defined by means of the trace

annotations.note: `Algebra.traceForm` is the symmetric bilinear form (x,y) ↦ trace(xy).

anchors.snippet: The trace form derived is a bilinear form defined by means of the trace

annotations.label: Discriminant of a number field

anchor.snippet: is defined as det( t )

annotations.note: `NumberField.discr` is the discriminant via `Algebra.discr` of an integral basis (determinant of the trace form).

annotations.label: Algebraic integer iff characteristic polynomial is integral monic

anchor.snippet: is an algebraic integer if and only if the characteristic polynomial

annotations.note: Integrality is defined via existence of a monic integer polynomial, but the specific characteristic-polynomial criterion is not a single named lemma.

annotations.label: Integral basis and discriminant computation

anchor.snippet: where x satisfies x 3 − 11 x 2 + x + 1 = 0

annotations.note: This specific integral-basis and discriminant computation is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: where x satisfies x 3 − 11 x 2 + x + 1 = 0

annotations.label: Place of a number field

anchor.snippet: is an equivalence class of absolute values

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `InfinitePlace` and `FinitePlace` separately rather than a single 'place = equivalence class of absolute values' object.

anchors.snippet: is an equivalence class of absolute values

annotations.label: Ostrowski's theorem for the rationals

anchor.snippet: the following non-trivial norms occur ( Ostrowski's theorem )

annotations.note: `equiv_real_or_padic` shows every nontrivial absolute value on ℚ is equivalent to the real or a p-adic one.

annotations.label: p-adic absolute value

anchor.snippet: the p -adic absolute value is defined by

annotations.note: `padicNorm p q` defines the p-adic absolute value on ℚ.

annotations.label: Ostrowski's theorem (places of a number field)

anchor.snippet: This statement is a theorem also called Ostrowski's theorem

annotations.note: Infinite places (via embeddings) and finite places (via `equivHeightOneSpectrum`) are formalized, but not packaged as a single Ostrowski classification for number fields.

annotations.label: Sixth root of unity field embeddings

anchor.snippet: provides a rich example for constructing explicit real and complex Archimedean embeddings

annotations.note: This particular ℚ(ζ₆) embedding example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Computing Archimedean places (real and complex places)

anchor.snippet: Calculating the archimedean places of a number field

annotations.note: Infinite (archimedean) places arise from complex embeddings and `isReal_or_isComplex` classifies each as real or complex.

annotations.label: Totally real and totally complex fields

anchor.snippet: is called totally real (resp. totally complex )

annotations.note: `NumberField.IsTotallyReal` and `IsTotallyComplex` formalize these properties via the infinite places.

annotations.label: Ultrametric (non-Archimedean) place

anchor.snippet: Such an absolute value is called an ultrametric , non-Archimedean or

annotations.note: `IsNonarchimedean f` means f(a+b) ≤ max(f a, f b), the ultrametric inequality.

anchors.snippet: Such an absolute value is called an ultrametric , non-Archimedean or

annotations.label: Ultrametric absolute value bounded by one on integers

anchor.snippet: For any ultrametric place v we have that

annotations.note: `intValuation_le_one` shows the v-adic valuation is ≤ 1 on the ring of integers, for any Dedekind domain.

annotations.label: Ultrametric place defines a prime ideal

anchor.snippet: For an ultrametric place v , the subset of

annotations.note: `equivHeightOneSpectrum` is the bijection between finite (ultrametric) places and maximal/prime ideals of 𝓞 K.

annotations.label: Prime ideal gives a discrete valuation

anchor.snippet: a discrete valuation can be defined by setting

annotations.note: `HeightOneSpectrum.valuation` defines the v-adic (discrete) valuation associated to a nonzero prime ideal.

annotations.label: Localization is a discrete valuation ring

anchor.snippet: is a discrete valuation ring , in particular a local ring

annotations.note: This shows localizing a Dedekind domain at a nonzero prime gives a discrete valuation ring.

annotations.label: Three-way equivalence of places, prime ideals, localizations

anchor.snippet: there is a three-way equivalence between ultrametric absolute values, prime ideals, and localizations

annotations.note: The individual correspondences (place↔ideal, ideal→valuation, localization→DVR) are formalized but not assembled into one packaged three-way equivalence.

annotations.label: Going up and going down theorems

anchor.snippet: are the going up and going down theorems

annotations.note: Going-up is `exists_ideal_over_prime_of_isIntegral` and going-down is captured by `Algebra.HasGoingDown` in `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.GoingDown`.

anchor.snippet: We say that an ideal

annotations.note: `Ideal.LiesOver P p` formalizes that P contracts to p (lies over p).

annotations.label: Lying over theorem

anchor.snippet: one incarnation of the theorem states a prime ideal in

annotations.note: This theorem provides, over an integral extension, a prime lying over any given prime.

annotations.label: Ramification

anchor.snippet: Ramification , generally speaking, describes a geometric phenomenon

annotations.note: The geometric description is informal, but ramification is made precise via `Ideal.ramificationIdx`.

annotations.label: Branched covering of the power map

anchor.snippet: has n points in each fiber over t

annotations.note: This geometric branched-covering analogy is not formalized.

annotations.label: Ramification indices

anchor.snippet: and numbers (called ramification indices)

annotations.note: `Ideal.ramificationIdx` defines the ramification index e of a prime in an extension.

annotations.label: Ramified prime

anchor.snippet: Whenever one ramification index is bigger than one, the prime p is said to ramify

annotations.note: Ramification index is formalized and `Algebra.IsUnramifiedAt` captures non-ramification, but there is no dedicated 'p ramifies' predicate matching this phrasing.

annotations.label: Ramification is a local property

anchor.snippet: Ramification is a purely local property

annotations.note: There is no single named theorem asserting that ramification is a purely local property.

annotations.label: Ramification index computation

anchor.snippet: In order to compute the ramification index of

annotations.note: This specific ramification-index computation example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Dedekind discriminant theorem

anchor.snippet: This is the Dedekind discriminant theorem

annotations.note: This states a prime p does not divide the discriminant iff every prime over it is unramified, the Dedekind discriminant theorem.

annotations.label: Galois group of cyclotomic field

anchor.snippet: of the cyclotomic field extension of degree n (see above) is given by

annotations.note: `autEquivPow` gives Gal(L/K) ≅ (ZMod n)ˣ for a cyclotomic extension.

annotations.label: Absolute Galois group

anchor.snippet: leading to the absolute Galois group

annotations.note: `absoluteGaloisGroup K` is defined as the automorphism group of the algebraic closure over K.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of Galois theory links fields in between

annotations.note: `intermediateFieldEquivSubgroup` is the order-isomorphism between intermediate fields and subgroups of the Galois group.

annotations.label: Kronecker–Weber theorem

anchor.snippet: By the Kronecker–Weber theorem , the maximal abelian extension of

annotations.note: The Kronecker–Weber theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (only listed as a target).

annotations.label: Hilbert class field

anchor.snippet: Also notable is the Hilbert class field , the maximal abelian unramified field extension of

annotations.note: The Hilbert class field is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brauer group

anchor.snippet: originally conceived to classify division algebras over

annotations.note: `BrauerGroup K` is the quotient of finite-dimensional central simple algebras over K by the Brauer equivalence.

annotations.label: Global fields

anchor.snippet: both types of field are called global fields

annotations.note: Number fields and function fields exist separately (`NumberField`, `FunctionField`) but there is no unifying 'global field' notion.

annotations.label: Hasse principle

anchor.snippet: The local-global principle or Hasse principle asserts that for quadratic equations, the converse holds, as well

annotations.note: The Hasse–Minkowski local-global principle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adele ring and ideles

anchor.snippet: the adele ring is set up. A multiplicative variant is referred to as ideles

annotations.note: `NumberField.AdeleRing` formalizes the adele ring, but the idele group (its multiplicative variant) is not a named declaration.
